structure.affirmative: Subject + had been + V-ing — I had been working. / She had been sleeping.
structure.negative: Subject + hadn't been + V-ing — I hadn't been working. / She hadn't been sleeping.
structure.question: Had + Subject + been + V-ing? — Had you been working? / Had she been sleeping?

usage.text: Activity in progress up to (and causing) a past event
Emphasising duration before a past moment
Past evidence explaining a visible past result
Unreal past conditions with duration (if … had been doing)

examples.post: for six hours when she finally took a break.
for two hours before the bus came.

faq.q: What's the difference between Past Perfect and Past Perfect Continuous?
faq.a: Past Perfect ('she had worked') focuses on completion of the action before a past moment. Past Perfect Continuous ('she had been working') emphasises the ongoing process and duration: 'She had been working all night' — the continuous effort, not just the fact it was done.

faq.q: Is this tense really used in everyday speech?
faq.a: It's more common in written narratives and formal speech, but it does appear naturally when telling stories: 'I was tired because I had been travelling all day.' It adds texture to past descriptions.

faq.q: Can stative verbs be used in Past Perfect Continuous?
faq.a: No — the same rule applies as for all continuous forms. Stative verbs (know, love, own) don't take -ing. Use Past Perfect instead: 'She had known him for years', not 'She had been knowing him'.
